output:
**[REASONING CONTEXT]**
Amplify: closed_loop_cycle_detection; stability_assessment
Suppress: loop_blindness; reinforcing_loop_tolerance — don't assume the system stabilizes neatly at the setpoint; track the overshoot caused by the delay.
**[END REASONING CONTEXT]**

## Simulation

Using **net +1.5°C/min** when heater is ON (as the problem specifies: 2.0 heating − 0.5 loss), and **−0.5°C/min** when OFF.

| Time | Temp (start) | Heater State | Event | Temp (end) |
|------|-------------|-------------|-------|------------|
| t=0 | 15.0°C | OFF | 15 < 20 detected → turn-on scheduled for t=1 | — |
| t=0→1 | 15.0°C | OFF | cooling | **14.5°C** |
| t=1 | 14.5°C | **ON** (delayed turn-on fires) | 14.5 < 20, no new trigger | — |
| t=1→2 | 14.5°C | ON | heating | **16.0°C** |
| t=2→3 | 16.0°C | ON | | **17.5°C** |
| t=3→4 | 17.5°C | ON | | **19.0°C** |
| t=4→5 | 19.0°C | ON | | **20.5°C** |
| t=5 | 20.5°C | ON | 20.5 ≥ 20 detected → turn-off scheduled for t=6 | — |
| t=5→6 | 20.5°C | **still ON** (delay!) | heating continues | **22.0°C** |
| t=6 | 22.0°C | **OFF** (delayed turn-off fires) | | — |

## Answer

**Yes.** The room reaches **22.0°C** at t=6, exceeding 21°C.

The overshoot happens because of the 1-minute thermostat delay. The temperature crosses 20°C at t=5, but the heater keeps running for one more minute (the delay), adding another 1.5°C. This is a classic feedback-loop overshoot: the system's response delay exceeds the time needed to cross the setpoint, so it inevitably overshoots by exactly **1.5°C** (one delay period × the net heating rate) above the trigger threshold, landing at 21.5°C + the 0.5°C it was already above when the trigger fired = **22.0°C**.
